references.notes: The term 'al-Manūn' refers to fate or death as an active, powerful force, a key cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'al-Dahr' refers to time or fate as an impersonal, irresistible force, a significant cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought.
The explicit mention of 'al-Jinn' refers to supernatural entities widely believed in during the pre-Islamic period.

The verb 'أحرم' (to enter a sacred state or zone, or to make something forbidden/sacred) implies a ritualistic or sacred prohibition, common in pre-Islamic Arabian practice related to ḥaram zones or sacred months.
'Amr Allāh' (the command/decree of God) refers to divine will or fate as a cosmological force, consistent with pre-Islamic notions of a supreme deity's decree.
'Al-Qaḍāʾ' refers to divine decree or fate, a significant cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought.
'ʿIlm Allāh' (God's knowledge) implies a divine awareness or judgment, functioning as a cosmological concept of divine oversight in pre-Islamic belief.
'Ḥilf Dhī al-Majāz' refers to an alliance or covenant made at the sacred market of Dhu al-Majaz, implying a solemn agreement often involving sacred oaths and a sacred location.
'Al-Qaḍāʾ' refers to divine decree or fate, a significant cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought.
'Al-Rabb wa al-Shahīd' (The Lord and The Witness) explicitly names a divine entity invoked as a guarantor or observer, fitting the category of deities and oaths.
This verse contains an oath formula swearing by the life of a father, a common pre-Islamic practice of anchoring an oath to a respected or sacred entity.
The term 'al-ḥāzī' refers to a diviner, a person who mediates the sacred through practices like geomancy, thus fitting a tribal sacral role.
The term 'al-shāḥij' refers to a croaking bird (like a raven or crow), whose calls were traditionally observed for augury and omens in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This phrase is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by the life of a respected person, which served as a sacred anchor for truthfulness.
The term 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) is personified here as an active, malevolent force, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological belief in Dahr as an indifferent or destructive agent of fate.
The phrase 'their sanctuaries for graves' refers to sacred spaces or niches associated with burial, indicating a pre-Islamic ritualistic practice or sacred geography related to the deceased.
The personification of 'the days' (الأيام) as an active agent that 'casts' misfortune reflects the pre-Islamic concept of time (dahr) as a powerful, often destructive, cosmological force.
The phrase 'stricken by dahr' (عقيرا للدهر) explicitly names 'dahr' (time/fate) as a force causing misfortune, a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept.

The 'event of time' (حادث الزمان) refers to the impersonal, often unavoidable, occurrences brought about by time, reinforcing the pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of time (dahr/zamān) as a dominant force.
This verse refers to 'the rising of the stars' (طلق النجوم) being perceived as an 'ill omen' (النحس), indicating a belief in astral influences and omens, which falls under cosmological concepts.
The term 'al-Ḥarām' (sacred, inviolable) is a key pre-Islamic religious concept referring to sacred zones or entities, here used in a tribal name.
The term 'aṣdāʾ' (plural of ṣadā) refers to the pre-Islamic belief in a bird-like entity emerging from the skull of the unavenged dead, here used in a place name.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is used in these verses to personify time or fate as an active, often overwhelming, force, which is a significant pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This verse explicitly invokes 'Zayd Manāt,' referring to a tribe associated with the pre-Islamic goddess Manāt.
This verse explicitly references al-Ṣafā, a sacred hill, and the pre-Islamic pilgrimage (al-Ḥajj) and its season (al-Mawsim), indicating sacred geography and ritual practice.
